request.question: Sorry is a 1966 song that was written by Stevie Wright and another Australian musician who moved to Australia with his family as a teenager, but was born where?

title: Chris Cheney
George Young (rock musician)
Sorry (The Easybeats song)
Black Eyed Bruiser (album)
The Wrights (Australian band)
Wedding Ring (song)
She's So Fine
Evie (song)
Harry Bailey
Hard Road (Stevie Wright album)
sentences: Christopher John Cheney (born 2 January 1975) is an Australian rock musician, record producer and studio owner.
He is the founding mainstay guitarist, songwriter and lead vocalist of the psychobilly band, The Living End, which was formed in 1994 with school mate Scott Owen.

Cheney wrote the group's top 20 hits on the ARIA Singles Chart: "Second Solution" / "Prisoner of Society" (1997), "All Torn Down" (1999), "Pictures in the Mirror" (2000), "Roll On" (2001), "One Said to the Other" (2003), "What's on Your Radio" (2005), "Wake Up" (2006) and "White Noise" (2008).
In 2004 Cheney joined the super group The Wrights which put out a cover version of Stevie Wright's epic 11-minute track, "Evie" as a single.
At the APRA Awards of 2009 Cheney won 'Song of the Year' for writing The Living End's track, "White Noise".
In 2005 he married Emma, the couple have two daughters and are co-owners of a recording facility, Red Door Studios.
In 2011 the Cheney family relocated to Los Angeles.
George Redburn Young (born 6 November 1946) is an Australian musician, songwriter and record producer.
Born in Scotland, he moved to Australia with his family as a teenager, and became a naturalised citizen.

He is best known as a member of the 1960s Australian rock band the Easybeats, and as a co-writer of the international hits "Friday on My Mind" and "Love Is in the Air", the latter recorded by John Paul Young (no relation).
Young was also the producer of some work by the Australian hard rock band AC/DC, formed by his younger brothers Malcolm and Angus Young.
"Sorry" is a 1966 song and single by Australian rock group The Easybeats, which was written by band members George Young and Stevie Wright.
It peaked at #1 on the Australian Go-Set's National Top 40 in mid November 1966.
It remained at #1 on the Australian Charts for 2 weeks in November 1966.
Black Eyed Bruiser is the second studio album from Australian singer Stevie Wright.
The album was not as commercially successful as its predecessor "Hard Road" and would be the Wright's final album released with production team Vanda and Young and record label Albert Productions.
The Wrights were a one-off Australian rock music "supergroup".

They consisted of Nic Cester (of Jet), Bernard Fanning (of Powderfinger), Phil Jamieson (of Grinspoon), Kram (of Spiderbait), Chris Cheney (of The Living End), Davey Lane (of You Am I and The Pictures) and Pat Bourke (of Dallas Crane).
They are named after Australian music legend and former Easybeats frontman Stevie Wright, the original performer of the song-trilogy "Evie" which was the group's feature song.
"Wedding Ring" is a song written by Stevie Wright and George Young.
It was originally recorded by the Australian rock group the Easybeats in 1965, whose version reached #6 on the Australian charts.
"She's So Fine" is a song written by Stevie Wright and George Young.
It was originally recorded by the Australian rock group the Easybeats in 1965, whose version reached number three in the Australian charts.
"Evie" is an Australian rock song released as a single in 1974 by Australian singer and former frontman of The Easybeats, Stevie Wright.
It was written by Harry Vanda and George Young.
It has been suggested that it is the first 11-minute song to chart at #1 anywhere in the world.
Harry Richard Bailey (29 October 1922, Picton, New South Wales – 8 September 1985, Mount White, New South Wales) was an Australian psychiatrist and hospital administrator.

He bore the primary responsibility for treatment of mental patients and drug dependent clients via deep sleep therapy and other method at a mental hospital in the Sydney North Shore suburb of Pennant Hills; he was its principal.
The treatment has been linked with the deaths of a total of 85 patients, including 19 who committed suicide.
Bailey committed suicide while he was investigated.

One of Bailey's clients was Australian rock and pop singer and songwriter Stevie Wright, who was being treated for methadone addiction and later committed suicide, a report that was later part of a 2013 documentary on "Australian Story".
Hard Road is the debut solo album from Australian singer Stevie Wright.
The album's first single "Evie (Part 1)" was hugely successful and the title track was later covered on Rod Stewart's 1974 album "Smiler".
The album itself reach #2 on the Australian albums charts in 1974 was the 16th highest selling album in Australia that year.
The compact disc is currently out-of-print and has become quite rare.
A digital edition was available on iTunes as of June, 2014.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: Sorry is a 1966 song that was written by Stevie Wright and another Australian musician who moved to Australia with his family as a teenager, but was born where?
hop1_docs: Sorry (The Easybeats song) | "Sorry" is a 1966 song and single by Australian rock group The Easybeats, which was written by band members George Young and Stevie Wright.  It peaked at #1 on the Australian Go-Set's National Top 40 in mid November 1966.  It remained at #1 on the Australian Charts for 2 weeks in November 1966.

Stevie Wright | Stephen Carlton "Stevie" Wright (20 December 1947 – 27 December 2015), formerly billed as Little Stevie, was an English-born musician and songwriter who has been called Australia's first international pop star.  During 1964–69 he was lead singer of Sydney-based rock and roll band the Easybeats, widely regarded as the greatest Australian pop band of the 1960s.

Kram (musician) | Kram (born Mark Anthony Maher in Finley, New South Wales) is the drummer/singer of Australian band Spiderbait.  His stage name is an anagram of his first name; "Kram" is simply "Mark" spelled backwards.  Kram has appeared at the 2004 benefit concert for the South-East Asian tsunami, WaveAid, as a member of supergroup The Wrights who performed Stevie Wright's 3 part classic, "Evie".

A Place in the Sun (Stevie Wonder song) | "A Place in the Sun" is a 1966 soul single by American and Motown musician Stevie Wonder.  Written by Ronald Miller and Bryan Wells, it was one of Wonder's first songs to contain social commentary.  "A Place in the Sun" was his third Top Ten hit since 1963 and hit #9 on the "Billboard" pop singles chart and # 3 on the R&B charts.  Stevie Wonder also recorded a version of the song in Italian titled "Il Sole è di Tutti" ("The Sun is for Everyone").

George Young (rock musician) | George Redburn Young (born 6 November 1946) is an Australian musician, songwriter and record producer.  Born in Scotland, he moved to Australia with his family as a teenager, and became a naturalised citizen.  He is best known as a member of the 1960s Australian rock band the Easybeats, and as a co-writer of the international hits "Friday on My Mind" and "Love Is in the Air", the latter recorded by John Paul Young (no relation).  Young was also the producer of some work by the Australian hard rock band AC/DC, formed by his younger brothers Malcolm and Angus Young.

Wedding Ring (song) | "Wedding Ring" is a song written by Stevie Wright and George Young.  It was originally recorded by the Australian rock group the Easybeats in 1965, whose version reached #6 on the Australian charts.

Evie (song) | "Evie" is an Australian rock song released as a single in 1974 by Australian singer and former frontman of The Easybeats, Stevie Wright.  It was written by Harry Vanda and George Young.  It has been suggested that it is the first 11-minute song to chart at #1 anywhere in the world.
hop1_titles: A Place in the Sun (Stevie Wonder song)
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
1) Evidence found:
- "Sorry" songwriting: "written by band members George Young and Stevie Wright." (Sorry (The Easybeats song) [1])
- Stevie Wright: "English-born musician" (Stevie Wright [2])
- George Young: "George Redburn Young (born 6 November 1946)" and "Born in Scotland" (George Young (rock musician) [5])

2) Ambiguities:
- The passages only show one candidate for the co-writer: "George Young (rock musician)" / "George Redburn Young" (passage [5]). No alternate George Young variants or disambiguation pages are present in the supplied passages.

3) Bridge entity / Missing fact:
- Bridge entity: George Redburn Young (as shown in the passages).
- Precise missing fact needed: the exact birthplace (town/city and country) of George Redburn Young. Passage [5] gives the country ("Born in Scotland") but does not show a specific town/city in the provided excerpt.

4) Retrieval direction (prioritized):
- Fetch "George Young (rock musician)" page — check the lede/infobox and the "Early life"/"Biography" sections for full birthplace (town/city and country). Example search: "George Redburn Young birthplace infobox".
- Fetch "Sorry (The Easybeats song)" page — confirm songwriting credit in the lede/infobox (to corroborate co-writer identity). Example: open lede and credits.
- Optionally fetch "George Young" disambiguation (if the first page is ambiguous) or related biography sources for the Easybeats members.
Final answer deferred until those pages/sections are retrieved.
hop2_docs: George Young (rock musician) | George Redburn Young (born 6 November 1946) is an Australian musician, songwriter and record producer.  Born in Scotland, he moved to Australia with his family as a teenager, and became a naturalised citizen.  He is best known as a member of the 1960s Australian rock band the Easybeats, and as a co-writer of the international hits "Friday on My Mind" and "Love Is in the Air", the latter recorded by John Paul Young (no relation).  Young was also the producer of some work by the Australian hard rock band AC/DC, formed by his younger brothers Malcolm and Angus Young.

Whitney Young Birthplace and Museum | The Whitney Young Birthplace and Museum was the birthplace and childhood home of Whitney M. Young, Jr., an American civil rights leader.  The simple wooden house in Shelby County, Kentucky, near Louisville, is on the campus of the former Lincoln Institute, an all-black high school that Young attended and Young's father led.
Redburn, Northumberland | Redburn is a village in Northumberland, England about 0.5 mi west of Bardon Mill.  It is situated about 4 mi south of Hadrian's Wall.

Redburn | Redburn: His First Voyage is the fourth book by the American writer Herman Melville, first published in London in 1849.  The book is semi-autobiographical and recounts the adventures of a refined youth among coarse and brutal sailors and the seedier areas of Liverpool.  Melville wrote "Redburn" in less than ten weeks.  While one scholar describes it as "arguably his funniest work," scholar F.O. Matthiessen calls it "the most moving of its author's books before "Moby-Dick"".

Rural Municipality of Redburn No. 130 | Redburn No. 130 is a rural municipality in the Canadian province of Saskatchewan, located in SARM Division 2 which is located in Census Division 6.  Southeast of the city of Moose Jaw in the south central region of the province.  The seat of the municipality is Rouleau.
George Westinghouse Jr. Birthplace and Boyhood Home | George Westinghouse Jr.  Birthplace and Boyhood Home

Infobox | An infobox is a template used to collect and present a subset of information about its subject, such as a document.  It is a structured document containing a set of attribute–value pairs, and in Wikipedia represents a summary of information about the subject of an article.  In this way, they are comparable to data tables in some aspects.  When presented within the larger document it summarizes, an infobox is often presented in a sidebar format.
hop2_titles: George Westinghouse Jr. Birthplace and Boyhood Home
response.summary_2: Born in Scotland. George Redburn Young is an Australian musician who moved to Australia with his family as a teenager and was a member of the 1960s rock band the Easybeats.
